questions.prompt: Why are XML tags the recommended way to pass content to Claude?

choices.text: They look more professional in code.

Claude is specifically trained on XML-tagged prompts. Tags delimit content unambiguously, let you reference specific sections in instructions, and reduce prompt-injection risk because tagged content reads as data, not instructions.

questions.explanation: Anthropic explicitly recommends XML tags because Claude's training reinforces their use. Wrapping user-supplied or untrusted content in tags like <user_input> or <document> tells Claude to treat it as data and makes prompt injection harder to pull off.

questions.prompt: Your bot has inconsistent tone. Your system prompt says 'Be friendly and professional.' What's the better fix?

choices.text: Replace the vague descriptor with specific rules: 'Use first-person plural. Use contractions. Never start with phrases like ''I understand'' or ''Great question.'' Keep replies under 4 sentences unless asked for detail.' Specific rules produce consistent behavior; vague descriptors don't.

questions.explanation: Claude follows specific rules well and interprets vague descriptors inconsistently. Convert each tone goal into a concrete rule the model can verify it's following. If you can imagine multiple outputs all satisfying your description, the description is too vague.
